sentiment.rationale: Encouragement to Frederick G. Williams as counselor in the presidency, promising blessings for faithfulness in the ministry.
'Of him unto whom much is given much is required'—stern accountability for church leaders with renewed united order covenant.
Brief, protective revelation ensuring widows and orphans are provided for from the bishop's storehouse.
Grand revelation on priesthood authority, oath and covenant, temple Zion, dark minds rebuked, and the promise that every soul who magnifies the priesthood receives the Father.
Warning about record-keeping and those who don't receive their inheritance by consecration—prophesies 'one mighty and strong' to set things in order.
Parable of the wheat and tares explained with encouragement to the saints as lawful heirs of the priesthood.
Stark prophecy of war beginning with South Carolina, spreading to all nations—judgment and calamity upon the wicked.
The grand 'Olive Leaf'—the light of Christ, resurrection order, kingdoms of glory, the school of the prophets, and the promise of Zion's sanctification.
The Word of Wisdom given as counsel adapted to the weakest of saints—promises of health, wisdom, and protection to those who obey.
Affirms the First Presidency's keys, declares the oracles are sacred, and gives practical counsel on translating, learning languages, and managing temporal affairs.
Brief revelation that the Apocrypha contains much truth but also interpolations, and should be read by the Spirit.
Two-verse revelation admitting Frederick G. Williams into the united order.
Profound revelation on the nature of Christ, intelligence, light, truth, and agency—rebukes leaders for not teaching their families, but soars doctrinally.
Commands construction of buildings in Kirtland—a house for the presidency and a house for printing—emphasizing holy ground.
Sharp rebuke for failing to build the Kirtland Temple—'ye have sinned against me a very grievous sin'—followed by detailed temple building instructions.
Brief land management instructions for Kirtland—assigning lots and managing the French farm.
Praises Parley P. Pratt's school in Zion, defines the temple as a place of thanksgiving where the pure in heart shall see God, then warns Zion will be scourged if she pollutes her inheritances.
Teaches the law of war and forgiveness—saints must renounce war and proclaim peace, forgiving enemies up to three times before the Lord will fight their battles.
Brief missionary call to John Murdock with encouragement and family counsel.
Reassures Joseph and Sidney that their families are well, promises them power of speech, and declares Zion shall be redeemed.

summaries.summary: The Lord calls Frederick G. Williams as counselor in the First Presidency, promising blessings for faithfulness in comforting the afflicted and caring for the poor.
The Lord renews the united order covenant, warns that much is required of those given much, and commands the saints to bind themselves together in the bond of charity.
The Lord declares that widows, orphans, and children have a claim upon the church for their maintenance from the bishop's storehouse.
Grand revelation on the priesthood: its lineage from Moses to Adam, the oath and covenant, the temple in Zion, condemnation for neglecting the Book of Mormon, and signs following believers.
The Lord warns about church record-keeping, prophesies 'one mighty and strong' who will set the Lord's house in order, and condemns those who don't receive inheritance by consecration.
The Lord explains the parable of the wheat and tares, identifying the saints as lawful heirs of the priesthood entrusted with gathering the elect before the burning.
Joseph Smith prophesies that war will begin with the rebellion of South Carolina and eventually spread to all nations, bringing sword, famine, plague, and earthquake.
The grand 'Olive Leaf' revelation: the light of Christ pervading all things, resurrection order, kingdoms of glory, the school of the prophets, and the promise of sanctification.
The Word of Wisdom is given as counsel: avoid wine, strong drink, tobacco, and hot drinks; eat herbs, fruit, grain, and meat sparingly, with promises of health and wisdom.
The Lord confirms the First Presidency's keys, declares the oracles sacred, counsels learning languages and establishing the school, and gives temporal management instructions.
The Lord explains that the Apocrypha contains both truth and human interpolation, and that those who read it by the Spirit may profit thereby.
A two-verse revelation admitting Frederick G. Williams into the united order established by the church.
The Lord reveals that intelligence is co-eternal with God, Christ received grace for grace until He received a fulness, and commands leaders to set their own households in order.
The Lord commands construction of specific buildings in Kirtland: a house for the presidency's work and a house for printing the scriptures, both on dedicated holy ground.
The Lord sharply rebukes the saints for delaying the Kirtland Temple, declaring it a grievous sin, then gives detailed instructions for its construction and purpose.
Brief instructions on managing land in Kirtland, assigning lots, and managing the French farm for the benefit of the church.
The Lord praises Parley P. Pratt's school in Zion, promises the pure in heart shall see God in the temple, and warns that Zion will suffer scourging if she pollutes her inheritances.
The Lord teaches the law of war and forgiveness: saints must renounce war and proclaim peace, forgiving enemies repeatedly before the Lord justifies their defense.
A brief call to John Murdock to preach the gospel, with instructions to leave his children in the care of the bishop and not fear the world.
The Lord reassures Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon that their families are well, promises power of speech, and declares that Zion shall eventually be redeemed.

crossReferences.note: Directly echoes: 'Unto whom much is given much is required; and he who sins against the greater light shall receive the greater condemnation.'
The oath and covenant of the priesthood—those who magnify their callings are sanctified and receive the Father.
Both address the Book of Mormon as containing the fulness of the gospel; the church is under condemnation for neglecting it.
D&C interpretation of the parable of the wheat and tares from Matthew's Gospel.
Fulfilled 29 years later when South Carolina seceded, beginning the Civil War—one of the most specific D&C prophecies.
Both describe Christ as the true light that lighteth every man who comes into the world.
Both command seeking learning by study and also by faith—a foundational LDS educational principle.
Both teach that intelligence was not created or made and is co-eternal with God.
'The glory of God is intelligence, or, in other words, light and truth'—parallels John's teaching on the Word full of grace and truth.
The temple dedication prayer (Section 109) fulfills the command given here to build a temple for an endowment of power.
Both promise that those with pure hearts and clean hands shall stand in the Lord's holy place and see God.
Both command loving enemies and praying for persecutors—the law of peace and forgiveness.

verses.reason: 'Unto whom much is given much is required'—one of the most universally quoted principles in LDS discourse.
'I, the Lord, am bound when ye do what I say; but when ye do not what I say, ye have no promise.'
'Widows and orphans shall be provided for, as also the poor'—establishes the church's welfare obligation.
'Whoso is faithful unto the obtaining these two priesthoods... and the magnifying their calling, are sanctified by the Spirit'—the oath and covenant of the priesthood.
'He that receiveth my servants receiveth me; and he that receiveth me receiveth my Father.'
'I will go before your face. I will be on your right hand and on your left, and my Spirit shall be in your hearts, and mine angels round about you.'
'Verily, thus saith the Lord concerning the wars that will shortly come to pass, beginning at the rebellion of South Carolina'—fulfilled 29 years later.
'Wherefore, stand ye in holy places, and be not moved, until the day of the Lord come.'
'Seek ye diligently and teach one another words of wisdom; yea, seek ye out of the best books words of wisdom; seek learning, even by study and also by faith.'
'Draw near unto me and I will draw near unto you; seek me diligently and ye shall find me; ask, and ye shall receive; knock, and it shall be opened unto you.'
'He that ascended up on high, as also he descended below all things, in that he comprehended all things, that he might be in all and through all things, the light of truth.'
'All saints who remember to keep and do these things... shall receive health in their navel and marrow to their bones.'
'Shall find wisdom and great treasures of knowledge, even hidden treasures.'
'Shall run and not be weary, and shall walk and not faint'—the iconic Word of Wisdom promise.
'The glory of God is intelligence, or, in other words, light and truth'—one of the most distinctive LDS doctrinal statements.
'Intelligence, or the light of truth, was not created or made, neither indeed can be'—the co-eternality of intelligence.
'I have commanded you to bring up your children in light and truth'—parental responsibility for gospel teaching.
'I gave unto you a commandment that you should build a house, in the which house I design to endow those whom I have chosen with power from on high.'
'Let the pure in heart... come up unto Zion... and the pure in heart shall see God'—direct promise connecting purity with divine presence.
'Zion is the pure in heart'—the defining statement of what Zion truly is.
'Renounce war and proclaim peace, and seek diligently to turn the hearts of the children to their fathers.'
The law of repeated forgiveness: the Lord fights the battles of those who forgive their enemies three times.
'Your families are well; they are in mine hands, and I will do with them as seemeth me good'—reassurance to worried missionary leaders.
